source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 14.5-14.5.2 module concepts (pp. 270-273)

questions.q: What do Application (Module) Streams let a repository do in RL 8.x/9.x?
options: Host multiple versions of an application and its dependencies, so you can pick a specific version
Compress packages more efficiently
Stream package downloads over the network
Replace the RPM database
questions.why: Modular Application Streams let a repo offer several versions (streams) of an app, giving admins version flexibility.

options: A specific version of the application within a module
A network connection to the repo
A group of unrelated packages
The GPG key of the module
questions.why: A module can contain multiple streams (versions) of the same application; after enabling a module you can use only one stream.

options: A set of packages bundled for a specific usage scenario (e.g. common, minimal, server)
A user account for the module
The module's changelog
The repository ID
questions.why: A profile is a named package set for a use case; a stream can have several profiles (or none).

questions.q: In 'dnf module list' output, what does the [d] marker mean?
options: It marks the default stream (or default profile)
It marks a disabled module
It marks an installed module
It marks a downloaded module
questions.why: [d] is the default stream/profile; the hints are [d]efault, [e]nabled, [x]disabled, [i]nstalled.

questions.q: How do RL 8.x/9.x differ from RL 10.x regarding Application Streams?
options: RL 8/9 provide them in modular form (dnf module works); RL 10 is non-modular (dnf module no longer works)
RL 10 is modular; RL 8/9 are not
Neither version has Application Streams
They behave identically
questions.why: RL 8/9 use Module Streams via dnf module; from RL 10, Application Streams are non-modular and dnf module is gone.
